card.intuition: During DFS in an undirected graph, if you reach a visited node that isn't your immediate parent, you've found a cycle. Alternatively, Union-Find detects a cycle when two vertices of an edge already belong to the same component.
card.analogy: Hiking a trail and tying string behind you: if you stumble upon your own string coming from a different direction, you've walked in a loop. The string from your direct previous step doesn't count.
card.time_complexity: O(V + E) for DFS; O(E · α(V)) ≈ O(E) for Union-Find
when_to_use: Checking if an undirected graph is a tree (connected + no cycles)
As a simpler alternative to 3-colour DFS when direction doesn't matter
gotchas: Pass parent to DFS to avoid treating the edge back to parent as a cycle — in undirected graphs every edge appears twice
For multigraphs with parallel edges, track parent edge index rather than parent node
Union-Find approach: a cycle exists iff an edge (u,v) has find(u) == find(v) before union

exercise.prompt: Detect a cycle in an undirected graph using DFS with parent tracking.
hints: If v is already visited AND v is not the parent of u, we've found a back-edge — return True for cycle
exercise.test_code:
# Tree — no cycle
adj1 = {0:[1,2], 1:[0,3], 2:[0], 3:[1]}
assert not has_cycle_undirected(4, adj1)

# Cycle: 0-1-2-0
adj2 = {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[0,1]}
assert has_cycle_undirected(3, adj2)

# Disconnected, one component cyclic
adj3 = {0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[3,4], 3:[2,4], 4:[2,3]}
assert has_cycle_undirected(5, adj3)

# Single node
assert not has_cycle_undirected(1, {0:[]})
print('PASS')
